notes.text: RENDER THE FAQ INTO THE ARTICLE BODY: You correctly set has_faq: true and populated faq_pairs in the YAML, but the rendered FAQ section never appears in the article body. Add a '## Frequently Asked Questions' H2 followed by each Q+A pair formatted in bold question / plain-text answer blocks. This is a hard checklist requirement and also a significant GSO (Google Search Optimization) opportunity given the FAQPage schema type you've declared.

VERIFY BOTH SOURCE URLs BEFORE PUBLICATION: The SiliconANGLE article URL (siliconangle.com/2026/03/28/agentic-ai-gap-vendors-sprint-enterprises-crawl/) and the AWS Security Blog URL could not be confirmed via search. If either URL is fabricated or a best-guess placeholder, this is a credibility-ending error for an executive audience. Manually open both links, confirm they resolve to the cited content, and add a third verifiable source (the JPMorgan reference is used inline but never sourced in the Sources section).

ADD AT LEAST ONE INTERNAL LINK: TOF articles require a minimum of one internal markdown link to an existing Particle Post post. Review published slugs in the content library and anchor a relevant phrase — for example, 'agentic AI governance framework' or 'enterprise AI strategy' — to a related article. Internal links improve dwell time, support topical authority clustering, and are a non-negotiable funnel compliance item for TOF.

CRITICAL BEFORE PUBLISH — Fix the H1 to a question: TOF articles require an interrogative H1 for both funnel compliance and GSO optimization. A title like 'Can a CFO Deploy AI Without a Chief AI Officer?' preserves the primary keyword 'CFO AI deployment' while satisfying the TOF structure rule. This is the single highest-leverage change you can make.

RENDER THE FAQ IN THE ARTICLE BODY: You have five strong FAQ pairs in the YAML frontmatter, but none of them appear as a rendered '## Frequently Asked Questions' section in the article body. Since has_faq=true and schema_type is FAQPage, the body must include the Q+A markup so both readers and search engines see the structured content. Add the section above Sources.

REPLACE THE ORACLE SOURCE URL: Citing 'https://www.oracle.com/' as a source for a specific claim about a 2026 Fusion ERP AI agent rollout is not defensible. Locate the actual Oracle press release, product documentation page, or earnings call transcript and link directly to it. A bare homepage URL will be flagged by any editorial reviewer and undermines the credibility of an otherwise well-sourced piece.

Replace the picsum.photos placeholder image immediately — this is a hard blocker. Source a real, licensed image from Unsplash, Getty, or your internal asset library and update cover.image with the permanent URL before resubmitting. Picsum images are randomly generated and will display inconsistently across page loads, which undermines publication credibility.

Convert your three prose internal-link references into actual markdown hyperlinks. You have the right instinct (referencing related Particle Post content at natural anchor points), but the links must use the format [descriptive anchor text](/slug-goes-here) to satisfy MOF's two-internal-link requirement and to pass Google's internal linking signals to those pages. Pull the correct slugs from your CMS or the YAML frontmatter of the target articles.

The 'author' field in the YAML frontmatter is blank. Every published piece needs a byline for E-E-A-T (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness) signaling to both readers and search engines. Assign either a staff author or a clearly labeled editorial team attribution before publication.

FAQ body rendering is required when has_faq=true: The faq_pairs block in the YAML frontmatter is correct, but the article body must also contain a rendered '## Frequently Asked Questions' section with the Q+A pairs written out in full. Relying solely on frontmatter data means the FAQ will not display if the theme's shortcode is absent, and it fails checklist item 14. Add the FAQ section explicitly at the bottom of the body, before ## Sources.

Strengthen primary source attribution on the benchmark figures: The $12.88 and $2.78 cost-per-invoice stats are attributed to 'Aberdeen Group/APQC 2025' but Source 1 in the Sources section links to a Quadient blog post, not a primary APQC or Aberdeen Group report. For a finance executive audience, citing a vendor blog as the authority for a headline benchmark undermines credibility. Either locate the primary APQC 'Accounts Payable Performance' report URL or add a caveat that the figure is aggregated via Quadient's summary.

The comparison table is strong, but the 'Agentic Capability' row needs a source citation inline or in the Sources section: the claim that Automation Anywhere uses a 'UiPath-class RPA plus LLM layer' is unattributed and reads as editorial characterization. Add a footnote or inline citation to an Automation Anywhere product page or analyst report to prevent a fact-check challenge from readers who know the competitive landscape.

Populate the 'author' field in the YAML frontmatter before publication. An empty author tag undermines credibility with executive readers and creates a schema gap since the article uses FAQPage schema — Google's structured data validator may flag an incomplete entity record.

The answer-first paragraph after the first H2 ('Five Preconditions That Determine Deployment Readiness') runs slightly long and opens with a conditional clause rather than a direct declarative answer. Tighten it to a crisp 40–60 word declaration — e.g., 'Five preconditions determine deployment readiness: clean baseline data, structured ERP fields, a documented approval matrix, a dedicated integration owner, and confirmed data residency controls. Missing any one of them will stall the pilot before Week 4.' This keeps GSO compliance clean.

The vendor comparison table is a strong differentiator, but the Zapier pricing entry ('~$20/month') is the only figure in the table without a source citation inline or in the Sources list. Either add a source URL for Zapier's pricing tier or replace the figure with 'Tiered SaaS (see vendor site)' to avoid an unattributed claim that a skeptical executive reader — or an auditor — could flag.

Flip the H1 into a question to satisfy TOF funnel convention and improve GSO/featured-snippet eligibility — something like 'Is AI Fraud Detection ROI Real, or Is the Arms Race Erasing It?' preserves the keyword 'AI Fraud Detection ROI' while signaling the article answers a specific CFO question.

Add at least one internal markdown link to an existing Particle Post article (e.g., a related piece on AI in banking or CFO strategy). This is a hard TOF funnel requirement and also supports site architecture — check the content library for a slug to anchor to a relevant phrase like 'generative AI in financial services' or 'CFO technology investment.'

The first H2 ('The Myth That Gets CFOs in Trouble') is doing good narrative work, but its answer-first paragraph opens with storytelling rather than a direct declarative statement. For GSO compliance, the first 40-60 words after any H2 should lead with the conclusion, not the setup — consider opening with 'AI fraud detection is not a durable one-time investment' before the HSBC/AXA proof points.

Fix the H1 to a question for TOF compliance. The current declarative title 'AI Agent Governance Framework for Enterprise Deployment 2026' fails the TOF requirement. A revision like 'Does Your Enterprise Have an AI Agent Governance Framework Before 2026 Deployments?' preserves the primary keyword while meeting funnel-type structure rules.

The $240,000 transfer scenario in the body is compelling and specific — lean into more named, dollar-anchored examples like this throughout. The lede is strong (Beam AI stat in sentence one), but the fictional scenario could be even more credible if attributed to a reported or composite case study with a named industry vertical and timeframe.

Source spot-check passed: Beam AI's 52.9% figure is verified (47.1% monitored = 52.9% unmonitored), and the Deloitte 2026 report is confirmed live and real. Oliver Wyman and Darktrace citations were not independently verified via search — recommend the writer confirm those URLs resolve to the specific pages cited before final publication.

CRITICAL — Fix the MIT attribution in the lede: The article opens 'MIT's Project NANDA analyzed more than 300 publicly disclosed AI deployments,' but Project NANDA is an MIT networking/AI agents initiative unrelated to this study. The correct attribution is 'MIT's GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025,' consistent with how the report is named in the rest of the article and in the Sources section. Change the lede before publication to avoid a factual embarrassment.

FAQ RENDERING GAP: The YAML frontmatter correctly sets has_faq: true and populates five faq_pairs, but no '## Frequently Asked Questions' section appears in the article body. The FAQ must be rendered as a visible H2 section with Q+A pairs in the body — not stored only in frontmatter — for schema_type: FAQPage structured data to work and for the checklist requirement to pass. Add the rendered FAQ block before the Sources section.

STAT-BOX SOURCING PRECISION: The first stat-box credits 'MIT GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025' for the 95% figure — confirmed accurate. However, the bar-chart shortcode at the bottom credits both 'IBM Institute for Business Value / PwC 29th Global CEO Survey 2026' for three separate data points that come from two different studies. Split these into two separate visual elements or add a footnote clarifying which figure comes from which source, so readers and fact-checkers can trace each number independently.

Render the FAQ in the article body, not just in YAML frontmatter. When has_faq is true, the article must contain a visible '## Frequently Asked Questions' section with each Q+A pair written out in full — likely as bold question followed by answer paragraph. The faq_pairs block in frontmatter is metadata only; it does not satisfy the body-rendering requirement that readers and search engines see.

Add a labeled 'Clear Verdict' or '## Verdict' section before the Sources list. The 'Proceed Only When All Four Gates Clear' section does the editorial job but lacks the standard label that readers skimming for a bottom-line recommendation expect, and that our BOF template requires for consistent funnel structure across the publication.

The 'author' field is blank in the frontmatter. Every published article should carry a named author or a publication byline. Empty author fields create credibility gaps for executive readers and hurt E-E-A-T signals for SEO. Either assign the byline to the staff writer or use 'Particle Post Editorial Team' as a fallback.

Convert the H1 to a question for TOF compliance. A strong candidate: 'Does Your AI Agent Governance Framework Have These 5 Controls?' — this preserves the primary keyword 'AI agent governance framework,' signals the listicle format, and satisfies the TOF question-H1 rule that currently costs you 10 points.

Fix the Cisco source URL in your Sources section. You're citing mcpmanager.ai as the reference for the 24% stat, but the primary source is the Cisco AI Readiness Index PDF hosted at cisco.com. Tavily confirmed the stat is accurate, but linking to a secondary aggregator instead of the original report undermines credibility with executive readers and may raise fact-checking flags in future audits.

The FAQ section is well-constructed with 5 Q+A pairs that directly mirror likely executive search queries — this is exactly the right approach for FAQPage schema. Consider tightening the answer to FAQ #4 (kill switch requirements) by leading with the actionable spec before the 60% stat, following the answer-first structure you use well elsewhere in the article.
